informal: Suppose $f$ is defined and differentiable for every $x>0$, and $f^{\prime}(x) \rightarrow 0$ as $x \rightarrow+\infty$. Put $g(x)=f(x+1)-f(x)$. Prove that $g(x) \rightarrow 0$ as $x \rightarrow+\infty$.
lean:
theorem Rudin_exercise_5_5
  {f : ℝ → ℝ}
  (hfd : DifferentiableOn ℝ f (Ioi 0))
  (hf : Tendsto (deriv f) atTop (𝓝 0)) :
  Tendsto (λ x => f (x + 1) - f x) atTop (𝓝 0)
reasoning: The Lean signature captures all the informal hypotheses and the conclusion. `DifferentiableOn ℝ f (Ioi 0)` matches "f is defined and differentiable for every x > 0". Since `Ioi 0` is open, `DifferentiableOn` on it implies `DifferentiableAt` at every x > 0, so `deriv f x` agrees with the true derivative for x > 0; `Tendsto (deriv f) atTop (𝓝 0)` therefore correctly encodes f'(x) → 0 as x → ∞ (only the tail behavior matters). The conclusion `Tendsto (λ x => f (x+1) - f x) atTop (𝓝 0)` is exactly g(x) → 0 as x → ∞. No extra hypotheses are imposed and none are dropped.
